name: Configure S3/GCS Remote State Backend
description: Set up a remote state backend with S3 or GCS for team collaboration, state locking, and disaster recovery.

steps.title: Create the S3 bucket for state storage
steps.description: Create a versioned, encrypted S3 bucket to store Terraform state files.
steps.command:
aws s3api create-bucket --bucket my-tf-state-bucket --region us-east-1
aws s3api put-bucket-versioning --bucket my-tf-state-bucket --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled
aws s3api put-bucket-encryption --bucket my-tf-state-bucket --server-side-encryption-configuration '{"Rules":[{"ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault":{"SSEAlgorithm":"AES256"}}]}'
steps.tip: Enable versioning to recover from accidental state corruption or deletion.

steps.title: Create a DynamoDB table for state locking
steps.description: Create a DynamoDB table to enable state locking, preventing concurrent operations.
steps.command: aws dynamodb create-table --table-name tf-state-lock --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST

steps.title: Configure the backend in Terraform
steps.description: Add the S3 backend configuration to your Terraform code.

steps.title: Initialize the backend
steps.description: Run terraform init to configure the backend and migrate any existing local state.
steps.warning: If you have existing local state, Terraform will ask if you want to migrate it. Answer 'yes' to preserve your resources.

steps.title: Verify the backend is working
steps.description: Confirm that state is being stored remotely and locking is functional.
steps.command: terraform state list && aws s3 ls s3://my-tf-state-bucket/global/

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Terraform Remote Backend Setup (AWS S3 + DynamoDB)

BUCKET_NAME="${1:?Usage: $0 <bucket-name> [region]}"
REGION="${2:-us-east-1}"
TABLE_NAME="tf-state-lock"

echo "==> Creating S3 bucket: ${BUCKET_NAME}..."
if [ "$REGION" = "us-east-1" ]; then
  aws s3api create-bucket --bucket "$BUCKET_NAME" --region "$REGION"
else
  aws s3api create-bucket --bucket "$BUCKET_NAME" --region "$REGION" --create-bucket-configuration LocationConstraint="$REGION"
fi

echo "==> Enabling versioning..."
aws s3api put-bucket-versioning --bucket "$BUCKET_NAME" --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled

echo "==> Enabling encryption..."
aws s3api put-bucket-encryption --bucket "$BUCKET_NAME" --server-side-encryption-configuration '{"Rules":[{"ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault":{"SSEAlgorithm":"AES256"}}]}'

echo "==> Blocking public access..."
aws s3api put-public-access-block --bucket "$BUCKET_NAME" --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true

echo "==> Creating DynamoDB lock table: ${TABLE_NAME}..."
aws dynamodb create-table   --table-name "$TABLE_NAME"   --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S   --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH   --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST   --region "$REGION" 2>/dev/null || echo "Table may already exist"

echo ""
echo "==> Backend ready. Add this to your Terraform config:"
cat <<EOF

terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "$BUCKET_NAME"
    key            = "global/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "$REGION"
    dynamodb_table = "$TABLE_NAME"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
EOF

echo ""
echo "Then run: terraform init"

faq.question: Why use DynamoDB for state locking?
faq.answer: DynamoDB provides atomic lock operations. Without locking, two people running terraform apply simultaneously could corrupt the state file by writing conflicting changes.

faq.question: Can I use GCS instead of S3?
faq.answer: Yes. Replace the S3 backend with: backend "gcs" { bucket = "my-tf-state" prefix = "terraform/state" }. GCS has built-in locking so no separate lock table is needed.

faq.question: How do I share the backend across a team?
faq.answer: Commit the backend.tf to version control. Each team member runs terraform init, which configures their local Terraform to use the shared remote backend. Ensure all team members have cloud credentials.
